outline.emotional_arc: The relief of school's predictable rules slowly gives way to sensing something unnamed shifting at home.
key_events: Started at Fircroft Primary on Burntwood Lane, Miss Okonkwo in Year 1, strict but fair, always smelled like coconut oil, had braids with beads that clicked when she wrote on the board
Learned to sit cross-legged, to line up, to put her hand up — the rules were clear and she liked them, the predictability of knowing what came next
Got a Moshi Monsters membership for her 6th birthday, became obsessed with Katsuma, spent hours on the family computer in the corner of the sitting room, the radiator knocking behind her
Dad still home, still rumbly, still the fun one, but his shifts were getting longer — more late nights, more dinners without him
Mum's face doing the tight thing more often, the held breath, the 'proboha' at the fridge louder
Started answering teachers in English without thinking about it, stopped answering Mum in Czech without thinking about it — the division widening without anyone naming it
Watched Horrible Histories after school every day, learned all the songs, 'the Vikings came across the sea' playing on loop in her head
new_or_changed_relationships: Miss Okonkwo — the first adult outside the family who made her feel capable instead of fragile, she didn't hover or check if Anna was breathing, she just expected her to do the work and Anna did
Marcus — still present but thinner, like a radio signal getting weaker, she didn't notice the static yet because she still heard the music
Klára — the anxious arms got heavier, the grip tighter, Anna started pulling away without understanding why, Mum's love was becoming something she wanted to escape from
outline.internal_state: School made sense. Home was getting harder to read. The flat felt like it was holding its breath and she didn't know what it was waiting for. The radiator still knocked. She still flinched. But now there was something else underneath the knocking — something in the pauses between sounds, in the way Mum and Dad looked at each other across the kitchen table, in the way the silence after dinner was different than the silence before. She couldn't name it. She just felt it like weather.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: The school day was the spine and everything else hung off it. Up at seven because Mum's alarm went off at six-thirty and Anna always woke anyway, lying in bed listening to the radiator decide if it was going to knock or stay quiet. Weetabix at the kitchen table, Mum standing the whole time like sitting down would mean something bad, the milk going soggy if Anna didn't eat fast enough, the spoon scraping the bowl in a way that made Mum's face do the thing. School uniform — the red V-neck jumper with the Fircroft logo that Mum ironed every Sunday while watching Czech television on the laptop, the pleated skirt that itched behind her knees, the black shoes from Clarks on Tooting High Street that Mum made her try on three times before buying. Walk to school with Mum, hand held too tight on Garratt Lane, the buses too loud to talk, past the Ladbrokes, past the charity shop that smelled like old ladies, all the way to Burntwood Lane where the school gate was. Assembly on Monday. Phonics every day. Playtime where she mostly stood near the fence unless Miss Okonkwo was on duty, in which case she'd walk near her without saying anything, just close, just there. Lunch — packed, always packed, Mum couldn't handle the idea of school dinners, didn't trust them, didn't trust anyone — a ham sandwich, a Frube, an apple that Anna never ate. After school: Horrible Histories on CBBC at 3:45, she could set her watch by it, the theme song in her bones, 'the Vikings came across the sea' on loop in her skull for hours. Then Moshi Monsters on the family Dell in the corner of the sitting room if Mum wasn't using it for her Czech shows, the computer whirring like it was alive and dying at the same time, Katsuma's face on the screen, feeding Moshlings, collecting Rox, the radiator knocking behind her and her not flinching because the game was louder than the pipe. Tea at 5:30 if Dad was home, 6 if he was late, 6:30 if he was later, and sometimes it was just her and Mum at the table with the TV on, some Czech thing Mum streamed, Anna not watching, not listening, not understanding, answering 'ano' when she meant 'yeah' and Mum's face loosening for a second before tightening again. Bath. Bed. Mum's hands washing her hair, the 'šššš' that meant worry not comfort, the water too hot because Mum always made it too hot like the heat could protect her. The radiator knocking goodnight. The flat holding its breath.

key_scenes.location: Fircroft Primary School, Year 1 classroom, the carpet area near the whiteboard
key_scenes.what_happened: It was maybe October, she thinks October, the weather just turning. Miss Okonkwo asked who could count to twenty and Anna's hand went up before she knew it was moving. Not because she wanted to show off — she didn't care about showing off — but because she could count to twenty in two languages and the English numbers were right there, ready, easy. Miss Okonkwo called on her and Anna counted, one through twenty, no mistakes, and Miss Okonkwo's beaded braids clicked when she nodded and said 'Well done, Anna' and it wasn't the way Mum said things — it wasn't worried, it wasn't surprised, it was just fact. Like of course Anna could count to twenty. Like Anna was capable. After that Chloe Brennan tried to count and got stuck at fourteen and Miss Okonkwo helped her and it was fine, nobody laughed, but Anna understood for the first time that some people couldn't do things and she could, and that was a different kind of safety than the kind Mum offered. Mum's safety was don't-fall-don't-break-don't-touch-that. Miss Okonkwo's safety was you-can-do-this-now-do-it.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Capable. For the first time outside Dad's arms. Like someone had given her a tool instead of a bandage.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The click of Miss Okonkwo's beads when she nodded — wood on wood, soft and rhythmic, like a gentler version of the radiator.

key_scenes.location: The flat above Ladbrokes, Garratt Lane, the sitting room, the family computer in the corner
key_scenes.what_happened: It was a Wednesday, she thinks, or maybe Thursday, it was definitely a school night because she was still in her uniform. She was on Moshi Monsters, feeding Katsuma, the screen glow making her face hot, the radiator knocking behind her and her not even noticing. The front door opened and Dad came home and the flat changed temperature — not real temperature, something else, something in the air. Mum's voice went high, Czech, fast, asking questions, and Dad's voice went low, English, short, answering them. The sounds of them in the kitchen, the words not mattering, the rhythm mattering. High-low-high-low. Then quiet. Then Dad appeared in the sitting room doorway and she looked up from the screen and he said 'alright, Munch?' and she said 'more' without thinking, meaning more time on the computer, and he said 'five minutes' and she could tell he wasn't listening, really listening, he was just saying the thing you say. Mum appeared behind him and her face was doing the thing and neither of them were looking at each other and Anna went back to Katsuma and the radiator knocked and the flat held its breath and she didn't know what was coming but her body knew something was.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: The static beneath the music. Dad was still there. Dad was still rumbly. But something was wrong with the signal and she couldn't name it because she didn't have the word for what 'less' felt like when someone was still physically present.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The glow of the Dell monitor turning everything blue-white, and the radiator knocking behind her like a heartbeat she'd stopped noticing.

key_scenes.location: Fircroft Primary School, the corridor outside the Year 1 classroom
key_scenes.what_happened: Parent-teacher evening. Mum came straight from work, still in her Primark uniform, the blue polo shirt with the tag sticking out at the back because she could never tuck it right. Anna wasn't supposed to be there but there was no one to watch her so she sat outside the classroom on the floor with a reading book she wasn't reading. Through the door she could hear Miss Okonkwo's voice, low and warm, and Mum's voice, high and Czech-accented, and the words were English but the rhythm was all wrong — Mum trying too hard, over-correcting, saying 'she is good?' like it was a question instead of 'she's doing well' like Miss Okonkwo probably wanted to hear. When they came out Miss Okonkwo smiled at Anna and said 'you're doing brilliantly' and Anna said 'thank you' in her English voice, the school voice, the voice that never slipped, and Mum's face flickered — not the tight thing, something else, something smaller — and they walked home in silence and Mum's hand was too tight on hers the whole way and Anna understood without understanding that she'd done something wrong by being good at the wrong language.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Guilt she couldn't locate. She'd done what she was supposed to do. She'd been good. But good felt like a door closing and she could hear Mum on the other side of it, knocking.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: Mum's Primark polo shirt, the tag sticking up at the back, blue cotton, the way it marked her as someone who didn't belong before she even opened her mouth.

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: He was becoming a radio signal losing strength — still broadcasting, still there, but the static was eating the music. The late shifts were the visible thing but the real thing was the way he came home and sat in front of the television instead of sitting on the floor with her. The way 'alright, Munch?' became a greeting instead of a question. The way Sunday Nando's started getting cancelled — not cancelled, rescheduled, and then not rescheduled, just quietly dropped like a stone into water. He was still fun when he was there. He was just less there. She didn't have the word for this yet. She would.

The anxious arms got heavier at the exact moment Anna started wanting to pull away, and the physics of that created something new — a tension, a resistance, a rubber band being stretched. Mum's grip was too tight on the walk to school. Mum's face was too tight when Anna spoke English. Mum's love was becoming something that smothered instead of warmed, and Anna couldn't say this because she didn't know it, she just felt it as the instinct to pull her hand away, to sit further from Mum on the sofa, to want the flat to be empty sometimes instead of full of Mum's worry. The guilt of wanting distance from someone who was clearly trying so hard — that was new too.

She became the first adult outside the family who treated Anna like she was capable instead of fragile, and the impact of this was enormous and invisible. Miss Okonkwo didn't hover. Didn't check if Anna was breathing. Didn't hold her too tight. Just expected her to do the work, and Anna did the work, and the simplicity of that exchange was like water after weeks of juice — too sweet, juice, you forget what plain tastes like. She started walking near Miss Okonkwo at playtime. Not talking. Just near. Like the fish tank at nursery but warmer.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to sit still, line up, put your hand up, wait your turn — the rules of school
knowledge_acquired.how: Year 1 with Miss Okonkwo. The carpet. The line at the door. The hand up, not shouting out. These weren't restrictions — they were architecture. She liked knowing what came next. The predictability was the opposite of the flat, where nothing knocked when you expected it to.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Moshi Monsters — Katsuma, Moshlings, Rox, the whole ecosystem
knowledge_acquired.how: Her 6th birthday, Mum and Dad got her the membership after she'd been begging for weeks because everyone at school was on it. The family Dell in the corner of the sitting room, the dial-up sound, the loading screen, Katsuma's orange face becoming the thing she thought about during maths. The collecting, the completing — it was the same pattern as the sticker books, the stones, the Peppa episodes. More. More. More. Done. Empty. More.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Horrible Histories songs — all of them, but especially the Vikings one
knowledge_acquired.how: CBBC after school, 3:45, every day, ritual. She learned the songs before she understood the history. 'The Vikings came across the sea' played in her head during silent reading, during dinner, during the walk to school. She'd hum them without noticing and Mum would look at her with that confused face and Anna would stop, embarrassed, like she'd been caught speaking English.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Reading — real reading, not just phonics
knowledge_acquired.how: Miss Okonkwo's reading corner, the baskets of books, the Biff and Chip series that everyone read, but also the non-fiction books about animals that Anna gravitated toward. She read above her level and Miss Okonkwo noticed and moved her up and it was the first time being good at something felt like freedom instead of performance.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Rules are good because they tell you what's coming
opinion_snapshot.because: School had rules and school made sense. The flat had no rules and the flat made her flinch. Cause and effect. Sit here, then line up, then go to lunch. Nothing knocked without warning.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Miss Okonkwo is the safest adult who isn't Dad
opinion_snapshot.because: She expected things instead of worrying about them. She didn't hover. Her beads clicked when she nodded and that was a good sound, not like the radiator. She made Anna feel capable and capability felt like the opposite of fragile.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: English is the school language and Czech is the home language and they shouldn't mix
opinion_snapshot.because: English worked at school. It got you praised. It got you moved up in reading. Czech worked at home but only just — it made Mum's face soften and then tighten, like the language itself was a reminder that they were different. English was the outside world. Czech was the inside world. The walls between them were the only thing keeping either one standing.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Mum's love is heavy and it's getting heavier
opinion_snapshot.because: The grip on the walk to school. The 'šššš' in the bath. The checking, the hovering, the way Mum watched her like she might stop breathing. It used to feel like safety. Now it felt like a weight she was supposed to carry without complaining. She didn't know how to say 'you're holding too tight' when the reason was love.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Dad is still Dad but the signal is weaker
opinion_snapshot.because: He still said 'alright, Munch?' He still came home. He still existed. But the late shifts, the evenings in front of the telly, the Sunday Nando's that stopped happening — something was draining out of him, or out of the space between him and Mum, and she could feel the static even if she couldn't name it.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Singing 'the Vikings came across the sea' under her breath during quiet times
slang_and_language.origin: Horrible Histories, CBBC, every day at 3:45. The song became a reflex, something her mouth did when her brain was elsewhere, like the 'proboha' that Mum said at the fridge.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Answering Mum in English without thinking, then watching her face tighten
slang_and_language.origin: The school voice got louder than the home voice. English was the language that worked — it got praise, it got results, it got you moved up in reading. Czech was becoming the language that didn't work, that marked you as different, that made Mum's face do the thing. She didn't choose to stop. It just happened. Like breathing.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Moshi Monsters vocabulary — 'Rox,' 'Moshlings,' 'Katsuma' — used like they were real things
slang_and_language.origin: The membership. The hours on the Dell. The obsession that was the same shape as every obsession before it — the sticker books, the stones, the Peppa episodes. Collect, complete, empty, repeat.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Alright, Munch?' — parroting Dad's greeting back at him
slang_and_language.origin: Dad's voice, low, warm, the rumble. She started saying it because he said it. Then she kept saying it because it made him smile. Then she kept saying it after the smiles got smaller.

cringe_moments.moment: She was singing the Horrible Histories Viking song out loud in the bath — full performance, hand movements, everything — and Mum opened the door and just stood there, and Anna realized she'd been singing in English, loud English, like she was showing off, and Mum's face wasn't angry, it was worse, it was that small hurt face, and Anna stopped singing and never sang in the bath again.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: She still can't hum around people. The song comes into her head and her throat closes. She thinks about Mum's face in the doorway and the guilt is still the exact same size it was then, hasn't shrunk at all, like it's preserved in something.

cringe_moments.moment: She told Aiden Walsh that her Katsuma was level 14 when it was actually level 9, and he believed her, and then he told everyone, and then Chloe Brennan asked to see and Anna had to pretend the computer was broken that night and she levelled furiously for two days to catch up and made herself sick staring at the screen.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: The lie came out before she could stop it — she just wanted to be the best at something, just once, and instead she was the liar who had to make the lie true. She still sometimes exaggerates without meaning to and then has to fix it in secret.

cringe_moments.moment: Miss Okonkwo asked her to count to twenty in front of the class a second time, for the visiting headteacher, and Anna counted to fourteen and then went blank, just empty, and a boy named Jayden Wright whispered 'she forgot' and Miss Okonkwo had to prompt her and the word 'fifteen' came out wrong, too high, too loud, like a question instead of a number.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: The first time had been perfect. The second time proved the first time was luck. She still doesn't like performing on command. She still hears Jayden Wright's whisper when someone's watching her do something she knows she can do.

outline.emotional_arc: The radiator pipe becomes a confessor — she hears what she's not supposed to hear and learns that home is a place with secrets.
key_events: The arguments started — low at first, just voices in the kitchen after she was in bed, then louder, then Czech words flying fast and English words flying back
She could hear them through the radiator pipe that ran from the kitchen into her bedroom wall — the same pipe the knocking came from, the same angry man, now carrying her parents' anger into her room
Started lying in bed counting the Czech words she recognised — 'proboha,' 'ne,' 'prosím' — trying to understand what they were fighting about, never quite getting there
Dad started coming home later, sometimes after she was asleep, sometimes not at all — Mum's face on those mornings was a closed door
Began drawing cats obsessively — terrible at it, kept doing it anyway, cats don't argue, cats don't cry, cats just exist and let you exist near them
Mum started sitting on the balcony smoking after Anna went to bed — Anna could see the orange glow from her window, didn't know Mum smoked, never said anything about it
The damp spot in the bathroom got bigger, the smell got worse, nobody fixed it, she started holding her breath when she brushed her teeth
new_or_changed_relationships: Marcus — she started waiting for the front door to open and feeling the disappointment before it came, he was becoming someone who might be there rather than someone who was there

Klára — Mum's crying started, just once at first, in the bathroom with the tap running, Anna stood outside the door and didn't knock, picked at the wallpaper near the frame, a small curl she worked loose over weeks
The radiator — it wasn't just the angry man anymore, it was the thing that brought the arguments to her, she started pulling her pillow over her head when it knocked
outline.internal_state: Something was wrong. She knew it the way she knew the radiator was alive — not because anyone told her, but because she could feel it. The flat was different at night. The kitchen voices went places she couldn't follow. Mum's face was doing the thing more. Dad was less. She couldn't say any of this because she didn't have the words for it — she had 'more' and 'man inside' and 'proboha' but she didn't have the word for watching your parents become strangers to each other through a pipe in the wall.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: Up at 7:15 because Mum set the alarm and the radiator hadn't knocked yet. Weetabix at the small table in the kitchen, the one with the wobbly leg Mum kept saying Dad would fix. School days meant Fircroft Primary, the walk down Garratt Lane with Mum's hand too tight around hers, past the Ladbrokes and the charity shop that smelled like old ladies. Year 2 with Miss Okonkwo — carpet time, phonics, the gold star chart on the wall, Anna's name never quite at the top but never at the bottom either. Lunch was packed: Marmite sandwiches on white, a Frube, an apple she usually didn't eat. Home at 3:30. CBeebies until 5. The waiting started then. The front door could open at 5:30 or 6 or 7 or not at all. Some nights he was there and some nights he wasn't and she never knew which until the key turned. Tea was whatever Mum made — pasta with butter, fish fingers, sometimes beans on toast if Mum was too tired for anything else. Bath at 7. Bed at 7:30. And then the listening. The radiator pipe ran through her bedroom wall and into the kitchen and the voices came through it like the pipe was a telephone. Low at first. Then not low. She'd pull the duvet up and count the Czech words she knew — proboha, ne, prosím — like they were stars she could name but not constellations she could read. The orange glow on the balcony sometimes. The bathroom tap running. The damp smell getting worse. Same shit different night.

key_scenes.location: The flat above Ladbrokes, Garratt Lane, her bedroom, the wall with the radiator pipe
key_scenes.what_happened: The first time she really heard them — not background noise, not the low murmur she'd been ignoring, but actual words coming through the pipe. Dad's voice first, the low rumble she usually associated with safety, but it wasn't safe now, it was hard and sharp. Then Mum's voice, high and fast, Czech words mixed with English, the rhythm all wrong. She heard 'proboha' and 'no' and something that sounded like 'Marcus please' but maybe it wasn't, maybe it was Czech, she couldn't tell. The pipe carried it all, the same pipe that knocked, the same angry man, and she lay there with her eyes open in the dark counting the words she knew like they were going to add up to something if she just got enough of them. They didn't. She fell asleep mid-argument and woke up to silence and Mum's face at breakfast was a closed door with no handle.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Terror without a name. The pipe had been a knocker, an angry man, a thing that made sound — now it was a thing that brought her parents to her room without them knowing she was listening. She felt like she'd been given a key to a room she wasn't supposed to enter and now she couldn't leave.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The cold metal of the radiator pipe against her ear when she pressed it to the wall to hear better, the vibration of their voices travelling through the copper and into her skull like they were inside her head

key_scenes.location: The flat above Ladbrokes, Garratt Lane, the bathroom door
key_scenes.what_happened: The tap was running. She got up for a wee — it must have been after midnight, the flat was that dark, the kind of dark that only happens when everyone's supposed to be asleep. The bathroom door wasn't fully closed and the light was off but the tap was running and she could hear something under the tap-sound, a rhythm that wasn't water, a catch and a release, and she stood in the hallway in her pyjamas with the fraying elastic and she knew. She knew it was crying. Mum was crying in the bathroom with the tap running so no one would hear. Anna didn't knock. She didn't open the door. She stood there and her hand went to the wallpaper near the doorframe and she found a small curl, a place where the paper had lifted, and she worked at it with her fingernail. Just standing. Just picking. The tap ran and ran. She went back to bed without weeing. The next morning the wallpaper curl was slightly bigger and Mum's eyes were slightly redder and neither of them said anything.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Helpless and intrusive at the same time. She was seeing something she wasn't supposed to see, hearing something she wasn't supposed to hear, and the not-knocking was its own kind of violence. She picked the wallpaper instead of knocking because picking was quiet and knocking was loud and loud got you noticed and noticed got you asked 'what's wrong' and she didn't have words for what was wrong.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The wallpaper curl under her fingernail, the thin dry curl of it, the glue taste on her finger when she touched it to her tongue without thinking, the sound of water running and something else underneath

key_scenes.location: The flat above Ladbrokes, Garratt Lane, the balcony visible from her bedroom window
key_scenes.what_happened: She woke up because the radiator knocked and then she saw it — the orange glow. Outside her window, on the balcony, a small bright dot that appeared and disappeared. Mum's silhouette. The glow moved to Mum's face and away, to her face and away. She was smoking. Anna didn't know Mum smoked. No one had ever said Mum smoked. There were no cigarette packets in the flat, no ashtrays, no smell except the damp smell, but there it was — the orange pulse in the dark like a heartbeat. She watched for maybe five minutes. The glow moved. The silhouette didn't. Then Mum went inside through the kitchen door and the orange was gone and the balcony was just a balcony again and Anna lay back down and stared at the ceiling and thought about the things grown-ups do when they think you're asleep. She never asked Mum about the smoking. She never told anyone. The orange glow joined the knocking radiator and the running tap as things she knew but didn't say.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Betrayal without understanding why. The smoking wasn't the problem. The hiding was the problem. The fact that there was a whole version of her mother that existed only after bedtime, only on the balcony, only when she thought no one was watching — that was the problem. If Mum had a secret life, what else was secret? What else was behind closed doors and running taps and orange glows in the dark?
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The orange glow appearing and disappearing, appearing and disappearing, like a lighthouse, like a signal from somewhere far away, the way it lit the edge of Mum's face for half a second each time before going dark

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: He stopped being the one who was there and became the one who might be there. The front door sound used to mean Dad, meant biscuits, meant the low rumble, meant 'more.' Now the front door sound was a question she asked herself every evening, and the answer was 'no' more often, and the disappointment collected like dust on surfaces she couldn't reach. He was still fun when he was there. He was just less there. She started protecting herself by expecting nothing and then he'd come home early with a magazine and she'd feel it all at once — the relief and the anger and the confusion of loving someone who was becoming unreliable. She didn't have the word 'unreliable.' She had the feeling of waiting.

The anxious arms got sadder. The tight face got tighter. Mum was becoming someone who cried in the bathroom and smoked on the balcony and said 'proboha' more often and bumped the fridge every day and never learned where it was. Anna's love for her mother became complicated — not less, but tangled with the knowledge that Mum was hiding things, that the flat had rooms Mum didn't let her into, that the woman who held her too tight was also the woman who couldn't hold herself together. The wallpaper curl by the bathroom door grew under Anna's fingernail and it was the only proof she had that she'd stood there, that she'd heard, that she'd chosen silence over knocking.

It wasn't just the angry man anymore. The radiator pipe was a wiretap, a confession box, a thing that carried the kitchen into her bedroom. The same pipe that knocked carried voices. The same metal that scared her with its fist-sounds now scared her with its whispers. She started pulling her pillow over her head when it knocked, not because the sound was loud, but because the sound meant something was coming — arguments, Czech words she couldn't translate, the sound of her parents becoming people who fought in kitchens after bedtime. The radiator was the enemy now. The man inside knew things she shouldn't know.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Arguments have a rhythm — escalation, pause, escalation, silence
knowledge_acquired.how: Listening through the radiator pipe every night for months. The pattern became predictable: Dad's low voice, Mum's high voice, a pause that felt like holding breath, then both voices at once, then a door slam or a long silence. She learned to track the argument's progress like weather, like she could feel the pressure dropping before the storm hit.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Grown-ups have secret lives that only exist after children go to bed
knowledge_acquired.how: The bathroom crying. The balcony smoking. The kitchen arguments. All of it happened in the dark hours when she was supposed to be asleep, and the discovery that night-time had a whole other version of her mother in it — a version that cried and smoked and said Czech words fast and angry — cracked something open. The flat had a day-self and a night-self and the night-self was the real one.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Drawing cats badly but obsessively
knowledge_acquired.how: She couldn't say why cats. They didn't argue. They didn't cry. They didn't knock like fists or glow orange in the dark. They just existed and let you exist near them, like the fish tank at nursery but warmer, furrier, something she could put on paper and control. Her cats were terrible — lopsided, too many whiskers, eyes that didn't match — but she drew them on every scrap of paper she found. The back of receipts. The margins of homework. A whole stack of cat drawings in her desk drawer. Miss Okonkwo said 'that's lovely, Anna' once and Anna knew it wasn't lovely, it was just what her hands did when her brain needed to be somewhere else.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Czech words she could count during arguments — a game that wasn't a game
knowledge_acquired.how: Lying in bed listening to the pipe, she started counting the Czech words she recognised. Proboha. Ne. Prosím. Počkej. She'd tally them on her fingers under the duvet, adding each one like a data point, like if she collected enough of them the sentence would reveal itself. It never did. She didn't have enough Czech. She had the sounds but not the meaning and the gap between them was the gap between her and her mother, between the flat and whatever country Mum came from, between knowing something was wrong and knowing what it was.

knowledge_acquired.thing: The damp spot is getting worse and no one is coming to fix it
knowledge_acquired.how: She brushed her teeth every night and the dark patch on the bathroom wall was bigger than last month and the smell was like Babička's basement and she started holding her breath while she brushed because the air felt wrong, thick, like breathing in the thing that was growing. She told Mum once and Mum said 'I know, I've called the landlord' and the landlord never came and the spot kept growing and the smell kept getting worse and this was how Anna learned that telling someone about a problem didn't mean the problem would get fixed.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: The radiator pipe is a spy, not just an angry man
opinion_snapshot.because: It carries voices. It brings secrets into her room. The knocking was one thing but the whispers are another — the pipe is how the kitchen gets into her bedroom, how the arguments cross the border of her sleep, how she knows things she's not supposed to know. The man inside isn't angry. The man inside is listening. The man inside is telling.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Grown-ups are lying when they say everything's fine
opinion_snapshot.because: Mum's face in the morning after a night of crying was always 'everything's fine, Anna' and it was never fine. The tight smile. The red eyes she pretended were from tiredness. Anna didn't believe her. She never believed her again after the bathroom door, after the orange glow. 'Fine' became a word that meant the opposite of fine.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Home is a place with rooms inside rooms that you can't see
opinion_snapshot.because: The bathroom with the running tap. The balcony with the orange glow. The kitchen at midnight. All rooms she wasn't allowed in, rooms that only existed when she was supposed to be asleep. The flat she lived in during the day was smaller than the flat that existed at night. The night-flat was bigger and darker and full of things no one would say out loud.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Cats are better than people because they don't have secrets
opinion_snapshot.because: A cat doesn't cry in the bathroom with the tap running. A cat doesn't smoke on the balcony. A cat doesn't argue in Czech through a pipe in the wall. A cat is just a cat and you can draw it badly and it doesn't care and it doesn't hide and it doesn't make you stand outside a door unable to knock.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: If she doesn't ask, she doesn't have to hear the lie
opinion_snapshot.because: Asking 'what's wrong' gets you 'nothing, go back to sleep' and that's a lie and she knows it's a lie and the lie is worse than not knowing. If she stays quiet and counts Czech words and picks at the wallpaper and draws cats, she can live in the space between knowing and being told. The space is thin but it's hers.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Counting Czech words during arguments — 'proboha, ne, prosím, počkej' — tallying them on her fingers like a secret score
slang_and_language.origin: The radiator pipe. The night arguments. The need to understand what was happening through the only method available — collecting the fragments she could name, hoping they'd form a picture. A survival mechanism disguised as a game.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'fine' with the same tight mouth Mum used — mimicking the lie without knowing she was mimicking it
slang_and_language.origin: Klára's morning voice after a night of crying. The 'everything's fine, Anna' that Anna started using at school when teachers asked how she was. She didn't know she'd learned it from Mum. She thought it was just what you said.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'The man inside' — expanded now to mean the thing that carries secrets, not just the thing that knocks
slang_and_language.origin: Her own construction. The radiator pipe had a man inside and the man knocked and the man also whispered and the man knew things. The metaphor grew because she needed it to grow — 'angry man' didn't cover what the pipe was doing now. 'Spy' was closer but she didn't use that word yet. 'Man inside' was enough.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Answering 'I don't know' when she meant 'I don't want to talk about it'
slang_and_language.origin: A defensive structure built from months of hearing things she couldn't process. 'I don't know' was safer than 'I heard you through the pipe' or 'I saw Mum crying' or 'Why does Dad come home late?' — 'I don't know' was the wall she built around the rooms inside rooms.

cringe_moments.moment: Drew a cat for Miss Okonkwo at Fircroft Primary and Miss Okonkwo put it on the wall and all the kids laughed because the whiskers were on backwards and one eye was bigger than the other and Thomas Barker said it looked like a monster and she wanted to swallow herself whole
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because it was supposed to be a safe thing, the cat thing, the thing her hands did when her brain needed to be somewhere else, and putting it on the wall made it public and public meant people could see it and laugh and she'd given them a piece of the private thing she used to feel calm. She never drew a cat for a teacher again.

cringe_moments.moment: Asked Mum in the morning 'why were you crying in the bathroom' and Mum went white and said 'I wasn't' and Anna said 'I heard you' and Mum said 'you were dreaming, go get your shoes' and they both knew it wasn't a dream and they both pretended it was and the lie sat between them at breakfast like a third person at the table
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because she broke her own rule — don't ask, don't get told a lie — and she got told a lie and the lie was worse than the silence would have been. She should have kept picking the wallpaper. She should have stayed in the hallway. She should have never knocked.

cringe_moments.moment: Told Elara Chung at lunch that her parents 'don't fight, they just talk loud sometimes' and Elara looked at her with this expression that was pity or understanding or both and Anna realised Elara didn't believe her and the lie was so obvious that even another six-year-old could see through it
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because she was trying to protect the flat, protect Mum and Dad, protect the idea that her home was fine, and she couldn't even sell it to a girl who still believed in the tooth fairy. The lie was that bad. Her face was that bad. She was that bad at pretending.

outline.emotional_arc: The flat splits in two — Dad in the spare room, Mum in the bathroom — and Elara Chung becomes the escape hatch from a home that no longer makes sense.
key_events: Dad moved into the spare room when she was 7 — 'I snore too loud, love, your mum needs her sleep' — she believed him because she wanted to, because the alternative was a thought she couldn't hold

The spare room door stayed closed. Dad's things migrated slowly — the blue Head & Shoulders in the spare bathroom, his football boots by the spare door instead of the front door, the gradual relocation of a person she'd assumed was permanent

Mum's bathroom crying became routine — not every night, but enough — Anna memorised the sounds: tap on, sniffle, tap off, silence too long, tap on again. She sat outside picking the wallpaper into a small bare patch shaped like a kidney bean.
Year 3 started, Mr. Barrett let them listen to music during art — she drew cats while Fleetwood Mac played from his phone and for forty-five minutes the world was okay
Met Elara Chung in Year 3 — they invented a secret language that was just English words backwards, 'nna' became her name in the language and it felt more like her name than 'Anna' did
Elara's flat was bigger, they had a Wii, Elara's mum made good noodles with sesame oil, and nobody cried in the bathroom and nobody had a spare room with a closed door
Told Elara that Dad moved into the spare room but not about the crying, not about the pipe, not about the arguments — kept the flat's secrets inside the flat where they belonged
new_or_changed_relationships: Elara Chung — the first person outside the flat who felt like shelter, who didn't need explanations, who just let Anna exist without asking her to translate herself, their backwards language was a country nobody else could enter
Marcus — the fun one became the far one, still her dad but now separated by a door in the hallway, she started knocking before going in, she'd never knocked on a door in her own home before

Klára — Mum started saying things she shouldn't, small comments about Dad that she'd take back or apologise for, 'he doesn't appreciate what we have' followed by 'forget I said that,' Anna learned to hear what Mum meant instead of what Mum said
outline.internal_state: She was living in two countries now — the flat where everything was cracking and Elara's flat where everything was fine, and she was the only person who held citizenship in both. The spare room thing was fine. It was just snoring. The bathroom thing was fine. Mum was just tired. The arguments were just disagreements. She told herself these things and the telling worked during the day and stopped working at night when the pipe carried sounds into her room and she couldn't pretend anymore.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: Wake up to the radiator knocking, always the radiator, pull the duvet over her head and wait. Mum's side of the bed was already cold by the time she walked to the bathroom — Mum slept on the edge like she might need to leave fast. Breakfast was always the same: Weetabix with the sugar on top, Mum's hand shaking slightly when she poured the milk, the fridge still in the same spot, still getting bumped. School was Fircroft Primary, the walk with Mum holding her hand too tight until the gate, then the release. Year 3 with Mr. Barrett, the tall one with the stubble and the phone full of old music, art on Thursdays which became the only hour that felt like breathing. Tuesdays and Thursdays after school she went to Elara Chung's flat — the big one above the betting shop on Tooting Broadway, the one with the Wii and the sesame noodles and no closed spare room doors. Elara's mum Lenka Chung — no, Lin Chung, she was Chinese, not Czech, Anna had to stop assuming everyone was — worked late so they had the sitting room to themselves, Wii Sports bowling for hours, Anna always played as the blue Mii. Wednesdays she was home, and home was quieter now, Dad's door closed, the spare room door, the blue door with the paint chipping near the handle, she started knocking before going in, 'Dad? Can I come in?' and the pause before he said 'course you can, Munch' and the pause was the thing, the pause was new. Dinner was the three of them but not together — Mum and Anna at the table, Dad on the sofa with his plate, the telly on, nobody talking. Sunday used to mean Nando's. Sunday now meant maybe Nando's, maybe Dad was on a late shift, maybe they went without him and the booth felt wrong with two. Bathtime, teeth, Mum reading a story, the radiator knocking, the pipe carrying sounds from the kitchen, the wallpaper patch by the bathroom door growing under her fingernail every night she sat there waiting for the tap to turn off.

key_scenes.location: The flat above Ladbrokes, Garratt Lane, the hallway outside the spare room
key_scenes.what_happened: The night he moved in. She didn't see him carry the pillow. She came home from Elara's and the spare room door was closed and Dad's slippers weren't by the front door anymore, they were by the spare room door, the blue door. She stood in the hallway looking at them. The brown leather slippers with the worn heel on the left one, Dad's left foot was bigger, he'd told her that once, and now the slippers were on the wrong side of the hallway. She knocked. She'd never knocked on a door in her own home before. 'Dad?' and he opened it and the room smelled like him, the blue Head & Shoulders smell, and his clothes were on the chair that used to have Mum's jumper on it, and he smiled and said 'alright, Munch, what do you think of my new den?' and she said it was cool and she wanted it to be true so badly that it almost was. He said 'I snore too loud, your mum needs her sleep' and she nodded because nodding was easier than the other thing, the thought that wouldn't form, the thought that was forming anyway in the place where the radiator man lived.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: The world shifted one inch to the left and everything was still there but slightly wrong, like a painting hung crooked, and she couldn't straighten it, and nobody else seemed to notice it was crooked, and maybe it had always been crooked and she'd only just noticed
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The brown leather slippers with the worn left heel, sitting outside the wrong door, pointing toward the spare room like an arrow showing her where Dad had gone

key_scenes.location: The flat above Ladbrokes, Garratt Lane, the bathroom door
key_scenes.what_happened: She couldn't sleep. The radiator knocked and the pipe carried sounds and she got up to find Mum — not to find Mum, just to walk, just to move, the flat felt too small at night. The bathroom light was on, the door not quite closed, the gap showing white tile and steam. The tap running. The sniffle. She stood outside and she could see Mum's shape through the gap, sitting on the edge of the bath with her head in her hands, and the tap was running and Mum was crying the quiet kind, the kind that doesn't count, and Anna's hand went to the wallpaper and her fingernail found the curl by the doorframe and she picked at it while she stood there, picking and listening, picking and listening, the wallpaper coming away in small damp curls, until the tap turned off and she ran back to bed. The next morning the bare patch was the size of a kidney bean. She covered it with her hand when she walked past. Mum didn't notice. Mum never noticed the walls.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: She was a spy in her own home, collecting evidence for a crime nobody had committed yet, and the evidence was all sound — the tap, the sniffle, the silence too long — and she filed it away where the radiator man lived, in the part of her that knew things she couldn't say
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The wallpaper curl under her fingernail, the slight dampness of it, the sound of the tap running and Mum's breath catching like a record skipping

key_scenes.location: Elara Chung's flat, Tooting Broadway, the sitting room
key_scenes.what_happened: The backwards language started with a mistake. Elara said 'pig' and Anna said 'gip' and they laughed and then Elara said 'what if we just talked like that' and it wasn't a game, it was a door. They sat on the carpet in Elara's room — the bigger room, the room with the proper bed frame not just a mattress on the floor — and they made it up. 'Nna' for Anna. 'Arale' for Elara. 'Emoh' for home. 'Dad' backwards was still 'dad' and that felt wrong, like the word wouldn't invert, like it refused. They whispered it to each other at lunch, across the table in the dinner hall, and nobody knew what they were saying, and the not-being-understood was the best feeling she'd ever had. Elara didn't ask why Anna needed a language nobody else could speak. Elara just made one. That was the thing about Elara. She didn't need the whole story. She just needed the part Anna gave her.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: For the first time since the spare room door closed she had something that was completely hers, something the pipe couldn't carry, something the radiator man couldn't hear, a country with two citizens and no borders and no bathroom crying and no slippers on the wrong side of the hallway
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The carpet in Elara's room, the orange one with the vacuum lines still in it, and the way 'arale' sounded like a real name, like it had always existed, like she'd always been 'nna'

key_scenes.location: Fircroft Primary School, Year 3 classroom, the art corner
key_scenes.what_happened: Mr. Barrett put his phone on the windowsill and pressed play. Fleetwood Mac. 'Dreams.' He said 'draw whatever you hear' and the other kids drew obvious things — stars, water, whatever — but she drew a cat sitting on a roof looking at the moon because the song felt like looking at something you couldn't reach. The cat was black. The moon was yellow. The roof was orange. Mr. Barrett walked past and said 'that's brilliant, Anna' and he meant it, she could tell he meant it because he didn't say it the way adults usually said things, he said it like the cat had surprised him. Forty-five minutes. The song repeated. She added whiskers. The radiator in the classroom didn't knock. Nothing knocked. For forty-five minutes the world was a cat on a roof and Stevie Nicks singing about thunder and the spare room didn't exist and the bathroom was just a bathroom and she was just a girl drawing.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: The specific peace of being nowhere, of being inside a song instead of inside a flat, the way music filled the space where worry usually sat and she could just exist without translating herself
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The smell of poster paint and the sound of Stevie Nicks singing 'thunder only happens when it's raining' and the wet brush in her hand making the cat's fur go slightly fuzzy at the edges

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: He became the far one. Not gone — the spare room was still in the flat, still just down the hallway — but separated by a door she now had to knock on, and the knocking changed everything. The pause before he said 'come in.' The way his room smelled only like him now, not like Mum, not like their shared life. The football boots by the wrong door. The slippers. The gradual migration of a person she'd assumed was permanent. Sunday Nando's became unreliable. He was still fun when he was there. He was just less there. She started preparing for the closed door before she reached it, already rehearsing the knock, already expecting the pause, and the preparation was its own kind of grief — the grief of adjusting to less of someone who was still alive, still home, still technically there.

Mum started saying things she shouldn't. Small comments about Dad that she'd take back or apologise for — 'he doesn't appreciate what we have' followed by 'forget I said that, I'm just tired.' Anna learned to hear what Mum meant instead of what Mum said. The bathroom crying became routine, not every night but enough, and Anna memorised the sounds: tap on, sniffle, tap off, silence too long, tap on again. The wallpaper patch grew. The guilt grew too — the guilt of sitting outside listening, the guilt of not knocking, the guilt of being the reason Mum was crying, maybe, possibly, she didn't know. Mum's love was still heavy. Mum's hands were still too tight on the walk to school. But now there was something else — the comments, the taken-back words, the tight mouth when Dad's name came up, and Anna became a translator of silence, a reader of what wasn't said, and seven was too young for that job but she didn't know how to quit.

She became the escape hatch. The first person outside the flat who felt like shelter, who didn't need explanations, who just let Anna exist without asking her to translate herself. The backwards language was a country nobody else could enter. Elara's flat was bigger and nobody cried in the bathroom and nobody had a spare room with a closed door. Anna told Elara that Dad moved into the spare room — 'he snores too loud' — but not about the crying, not about the pipe, not about the arguments the radiator carried into her bedroom. She kept the flat's secrets inside the flat where they belonged. Elara never pushed. That was the thing. Elara just made space and Anna filled it or didn't, and the not-having-to-explain was the closest thing to peace she'd felt since the slippers moved.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to hear what people mean instead of what they say
knowledge_acquired.how: Mum's taken-back comments. 'He doesn't appreciate what we have' followed by 'forget I said that.' The way Mum's mouth went tight when Dad's name came up. The way Dad said 'alright, Munch?' like a greeting instead of a question. The bathroom sounds that meant sad, the tap that meant crying, the silence too long that meant Mum was trying to stop.

knowledge_acquired.thing: A secret language that belongs to only two people
knowledge_acquired.how: Elara Chung said 'gip' instead of 'pig' by accident and they built a whole country from that mistake. 'Nna' for Anna. 'Arale' for Elara. 'Emoh' for home. The language existed nowhere else. Nobody could enter it. Not the radiator man, not the spare room door, not Mum's tight mouth, not the pipe carrying kitchen arguments into her bedroom.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Closed doors mean something even when nobody says what
knowledge_acquired.how: The spare room door. The knocking. The pause before 'come in.' The way Dad's things migrated slowly — the blue Head & Shoulders, the football boots, the slippers — and each relocated object was a small proof that she was right to feel the shift, that the world had moved one inch and she hadn't imagined it.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Fleetwood Mac is what peace sounds like
knowledge_acquired.how: Mr. Barrett's phone on the windowsill. 'Dreams.' Forty-five minutes of art where nothing knocked and nothing cried and she was just a girl drawing a cat on a roof. She went home and found 'Rumours' on the family computer, played it quiet through the bad speakers, and the songs became hers, became the thing she put on when the pipe got loud.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: The spare room thing is fine, it's just snoring
opinion_snapshot.because: Dad said so. Dad said it and she needs it to be true because the alternative is a crack that might become a break and she can't hold the flat together if she admits it's already splitting

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Elara's flat is safer than hers
opinion_snapshot.because: Nobody cries in the bathroom at Elara's. Nobody has a spare room with a closed door. Elara's mum makes noodles and smiles and the flat smells like sesame oil instead of damp. The comparison isn't fair but she makes it anyway, every time she walks through Elara's door.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Grown-ups lie to protect you from truths you already know
opinion_snapshot.because: The snoring excuse. The 'I'm just tired' excuse. The 'forget I said that' taken-back comments. They think she can't tell. She can always tell. The lie is supposed to be a blanket but it's more like a sheet — thin enough to see through, thin enough to know what's underneath.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Music can fill a room the same way a person can
opinion_snapshot.because: Fleetwood Mac in Mr. Barrett's classroom. The way the songs pushed out the worry for forty-five minutes. The family computer playing 'Dreams' while she lay on the sitting room floor. When the flat felt empty she could fill it with sound and the sound was better than silence because silence was where the pipe lived.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: The backwards language — 'nna' for Anna, 'arale' for Elara, 'emoh' for home
slang_and_language.origin: Elara Chung's accidental 'gip' instead of 'pig' in the sitting room of her flat, the game that became a country, the language that kept the flat's secrets out because the secrets couldn't be translated backwards

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'it's fine' about things that are not fine, flat voice, no eye contact
slang_and_language.origin: The spare room. The snoring excuse. The taken-back comments. She learned to close her own doors this way — not physical doors, but the doors to her face, her voice, the place where worry lived. 'It's fine' was the English version of Mum's 'proboha,' something you said to fill the space where honesty should go.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Dropping Czech words into English sentences without noticing — 'prosím' instead of 'please,' 'ano' instead of 'yes'
slang_and_language.origin: The flat was more Czech now that Dad was in the spare room. Mum's relief at not having to perform English in the evening. The Czech returned when Dad left the room and Anna absorbed it like the wallpaper absorbed damp, without choosing to, without knowing it was happening.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Calling Dad's room 'the spare room' instead of 'Dad's room'
slang_and_language.origin: Because it was the spare room first. Because calling it 'Dad's room' would make it permanent and she wasn't ready for permanent. Because 'the spare room' implied he could move back, that the slippers could return to the front door, that the migration might reverse.

cringe_moments.moment: She told Elara Chung her dad moved into the spare room because he snores too loud and Elara said 'my dad snores and he still sleeps with my mum' and Anna didn't know what to say so she said 'well my dad snores LOUDER' and it came out defensive and high-pitched and Elara just looked at her and Anna changed the subject to the Wii and they never talked about it again
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because Elara's face did the thing — not pity, not exactly, but the knowing look, the look that said 'I know that's not the real reason' — and Anna couldn't admit it because admitting it would make it real, and she wasn't ready for real, she was only seven, seven-year-olds shouldn't have to hold the word 'separated' in their mouths

cringe_moments.moment: She knocked on the spare room door and Dad didn't answer for a long time and she knocked again and again and said 'Dad? Dad?' in that voice, the small voice, the voice she hated, and when he finally opened the door he was on the phone and he held up one finger and mouthed 'one minute' and closed the door again with her still standing there
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: The finger. The one minute. The door closing. She was a visitor in her own father's room. She was someone who had to wait. She went back to the sitting room and sat on the sofa and didn't cry because she'd decided crying should be loud and this hurt too quietly for loud.

cringe_moments.moment: At school they were drawing families and she drew the flat — Mum at the table, Dad in the spare room, herself in the hallway between — and Mr. Barrett said 'that's an interesting layout, Anna' and she realised she'd shown too much and said 'it's a mansion, they all have their own rooms' and the lie was so obvious that Sophie Chambers laughed and Anna wanted to eat the paper
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: The lie was see-through. Sophie Chambers's laugh. The way the drawing told the truth she'd been trying not to tell. She tore it up and threw it in the bin and told herself it was because it was rubbish, not because it was real.

outline.emotional_arc: The Saturday the world broke open — from not-crying to crying, from having a dad to visiting one, from home to the place where home used to be.
key_events: A Saturday morning in April, she was watching Horrible Histories on the sitting room floor, the Stone Age song, Dad came in with a bag — one bag, just one, not enough for a person who was staying
He knelt down. He said he'd see her soon. He said he loved her. She nodded. She didn't cry. Not yet. Crying was for later, for 3am, for the pillow, for when nobody could see.
The door closed. The flat was quieter than it had ever been. The radiator knocked and for the first time it didn't scare her because the thing she'd actually been afraid of had already happened.
Mum came out of the kitchen and her face was doing something Anna had never seen before — not tight, not anxious, but collapsed, like a building that had been holding itself up and finally stopped

At school on Monday she told Elara, just said 'my dad left,' and Elara didn't say anything stupid, didn't say 'are you okay' or 'I'm sorry,' just sat next to her at lunch for a week and that was enough
Elara gave her a friendship bracelet the day after — orange and purple thread, she wore it until the string rotted off her wrist, didn't take it off for baths or PE or swimming

Mum started crying openly now, not in the bathroom with the tap running, but in the kitchen while cooking, in the sitting room while watching TV, the silent crying that Anna hated because you couldn't hear it coming
new_or_changed_relationships: Marcus — became someone who visited, the Sunday Nando's started, same booth, he ordered too much, checked his phone under the table, tried too hard to be fun, she waited for him to forget to come and he never did but the fear was there every Sunday morning like a stone in her stomach

Klára — the dam broke, Mum was no longer hiding the grief, she told Anna things she shouldn't have, 'Dad chose her over us,' then apologised, then said it again a week later, Anna became the container for Mum's anger and didn't know how to close the lid

Elara Chung — became the only person who knew, not the details, but the shape of the thing, and that was enough — Elara was the escape and the constant and the friendship bracelet rotting on her wrist that she would never cut off
outline.internal_state: She didn't cry when he left because she'd decided a long time ago that crying should be loud or it doesn't count, and she couldn't be loud in front of him, couldn't give him that. So she nodded. And then that night the crying came and it was loud, loud enough that Mum came in and held her and they were both crying and the radiator was knocking and the flat was full of noise and none of it meant anything because he wasn't there to hear it. She kept thinking she'd done something wrong. Kept going through the list — was she too loud, was she too much, was the 'more' too much, did she ask for too many things. She couldn't find the reason but she kept looking.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: The mornings changed first. Used to be the rumble through the wall, the bathroom door, the Head & Shoulders smell drifting down the hallway. Now the bathroom was free at 7am and Mum used it and the flat smelled like Mum's shampoo — the orange L'Oréal one from Sainsbury's — and that was wrong, that was a small wrong inside a big wrong, but she noticed the small ones more. School days: cornflakes at 7:15, Mum's face doing the thing where she was trying to look normal, walking to Fircroft Primary by 8:30, Mum's hand too tight on hers the whole way. The school run got longer because Mum walked slower, or maybe time got slower, Anna couldn't tell. At school she was fine — not fine, but functioning — Miss Okonkwo's class, carpet time, lunch with Elara Chung who didn't say anything stupid, afternoon, home. The flat was different at 3:30. Quieter. The radiator still knocked but it didn't carry voices anymore, just heat. Mum started cooking earlier, standing at the stove longer, the same meals on rotation — chicken and rice, pasta with jar sauce, sausages and mash. The TV was on more. Mum let her watch more TV now, like screen time rules had evaporated with the spare room door closing. Horrible Histories on loop. The Stone Age song made her stomach hurt but she watched it anyway. Saturdays changed the most. The Sainsbury's routine with Mum stayed but the booth in the café felt bigger, the magazine felt like less of a prize. Sundays became Nando's days — Dad days — and Sunday mornings were a new kind of waiting, checking the clock, checking the window, checking if he'd text to cancel. He never cancelled. The fear didn't care.

key_scenes.location: The flat above Ladbrokes, Garratt Lane, the sitting room floor
key_scenes.what_happened: Saturday morning, April, she was on the floor watching Horrible Histories — the Stone Age song, the one with the cavemen, she can still hear it if she thinks about it — and Dad came in from the hallway with a bag. One bag. A blue holdall from the cupboard under the stairs. Not a suitcase. Not boxes. One bag that meant not-enough-to-stay. He knelt down on the carpet next to her and the TV was still on and the Stone Age people were still singing and he said he'd see her soon. He said he loved her. She nodded. She didn't cry because crying had to be loud and she couldn't be loud in front of him, couldn't give him that, couldn't let him see what he was taking. He touched her hair once, stood up, and the front door closed and the flat went quiet in a way it had never been quiet before, like the building itself had exhaled.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Nothing. Then everything. Then nothing again. The nothing was deliberate — she chose it, she held it, she made herself a statue on the carpet because if she moved she'd break and if she broke he might hear from the stairwell and come back and she didn't know if she wanted him to come back or if wanting that was weak. The everything came later at 3am with the pillow over her face and the sound that came out of her wasn't crying, it was something being ripped.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The carpet fibres against her cheek where she pressed her face down to stop the crying, the synthetic scratch of it, and the Stone Age song still playing, 'we're the Stone Age people,' and the song didn't know, the song didn't stop, the world didn't stop

key_scenes.location: Fircroft Primary School, the corridor outside the Year 3 classroom
key_scenes.what_happened: Monday morning. She found Elara by the water fountains near the Year 3 corridor and said 'my dad left' like she was reading it off a list, like the words were someone else's, and Elara didn't say 'are you okay' and didn't say 'I'm sorry' and didn't do the face that adults did — the soft face, the careful face — she just stood there for a second and then said 'do you want to play tag at lunch' and Anna said 'okay' and that was it. Elara sat next to her at lunch every day that week without asking why, without making it a thing, and on Tuesday she brought a friendship bracelet — orange and purple thread twisted together — and held it out without ceremony, and Anna put it on and didn't take it off for months, not for baths, not for PE, not for swimming at the Tooting Leisure Centre, the string went grey and fuzzy and the colours bled when it got wet and she didn't care, she would have let it rot into her skin.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Relief that someone knew without making her explain it. The bracelet was a weight on her wrist that meant someone had thought about her when she wasn't there, and that was the opposite of what Dad had done, and she needed the opposite right now, needed it on her body where she could feel it.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The wet thread against her wrist after swimming, the way the orange had bled into the purple, the smell of chlorine mixed with the cheap thread, the weight of it, she'd never had something that meant so little and so much

key_scenes.location: Nando's on Garratt Lane, the booth by the window
key_scenes.what_happened: The first Sunday. He picked her up at 11 and she was ready by 9, had been ready by 9, had been sitting on the sofa in her good jeans and the top with the butterfly on it waiting for the door. He drove to Nando's — same one, same booth — and ordered a whole chicken and two sides and a dessert and a Fanta for her and a beer for himself and she could tell he was trying, the way he kept asking questions, 'how's school, how's Mum, what are you watching,' and she gave short answers because the long answers were in a language she couldn't speak in front of him yet. He checked his phone under the table, the screen lighting up his chin, and she watched him check it and didn't say anything and he didn't notice her watching. The chicken was too much. He always ordered too much. The booth was the same. The Nando's was the same. Nothing was the same. He drove her back at 2pm and she said bye at the door and he waited until she was inside and she turned around at the top of the stairs and he was still there, still waiting, and she didn't know if that was love or guilt and she wouldn't know for years.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: The stone in her stomach that formed Sunday morning and didn't dissolve until Monday. The performance of being fine, of being the kid who was handling it, of not crying, of eating the chicken, of laughing at his jokes. The exhaustion after, like she'd run a race, like the whole two hours was a race she had to win by being normal, by being okay, by being the daughter who didn't make him feel bad for leaving.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The red of the Nando's menu under the overhead lights, the way the light made everything look like it was under surveillance, and the smell of peri-peri that would mean Sunday and Dad and trying for years after

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: He became someone who visited. The distance changed from down-the-hall to across-London, and the man who arrived for Sunday Nando's was trying so hard to be the fun one that he was performing, and she could feel the performance even if she couldn't name it. He still ordered too much. He still said 'alright, Munch?' But the ease was gone — replaced by effort, by checking his phone, by the way he asked about Mum and then didn't listen to the answer. She started protecting herself by expecting less, by not hoping, by treating Sundays like appointments instead of visits. The fear that he'd forget to come lived in her stomach every Sunday morning and never left even though he never forgot, not once, and she couldn't explain why the fear didn't go away when the evidence said it should.

The dam broke. Mum stopped hiding in the bathroom with the tap running and started crying in the kitchen, the sitting room, the car, like the structure that had been holding her grief back had collapsed with the front door closing. She told Anna things — 'Dad chose his freedom over us,' 'he doesn't care about anyone but himself,' then 'I shouldn't say that, forget it, I'm sorry,' then the same things again a week later. Anna became the container for Mum's anger, the receiver of words that should have been said to a therapist or a friend or a wall, not to an eight-year-old who couldn't close the lid on what she'd heard. The Saturday routine stayed but Mum's face in the Sainsbury's café was looser in a bad way now, not relaxed but emptied, and the magazine felt like a consolation prize instead of a treat.

She became the only person outside the flat who knew the shape of the thing, not the details — not about the crying or the radiator or the spare room — but the fact of it, the before and after. The friendship bracelet was a contract written in thread: I know and I'm here and I won't make you explain. Anna didn't have to perform okay-ness with Elara the way she did at home with Mum or at Nando's with Dad. Elara made space and Anna filled it or didn't, and the not-having-to-perform was the only place she could breathe.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Leaving is a verb that happens to you, not with you
knowledge_acquired.how: Dad didn't ask. Didn't sit her down and explain. Just left, with one bag, on a Saturday, while the Stone Age song played. She learned that the people who love you can make the biggest decisions about your life without including you in the conversation. This knowledge went in deep and stayed.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Grief has a schedule and she couldn't control it
knowledge_acquired.how: The crying came at 3am, not when she chose, not when it made sense, but when her body couldn't hold it anymore. She'd decided crying should be loud but the loud only came in the dark, in the pillow, when Mum came in and they were both loud together and it didn't help, it just meant two people were sad instead of one.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Your presence can be a burden you didn't ask to be
knowledge_acquired.how: Mum's words — 'Dad chose his freedom over us' — had a 'us' in them, and the 'us' meant Anna was part of what Dad left, part of the weight, part of the reason. Mum didn't say 'you' but Anna heard it anyway, heard that her existence was part of the equation, and the guilt of that was a new thing that didn't have a name but lived in her chest next to the sadness.

knowledge_acquired.thing: The waiting is worse than the thing
knowledge_acquired.how: Sunday mornings were a slow climb toward the door, every minute a check of the clock, every car sound a possible Dad, and the relief when he arrived was smaller than the fear had been, and the fear started again the moment he left because next Sunday was already coming.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: She made him leave by wanting too much
opinion_snapshot.because: The 'more' word. The biscuits. The stickers. The Nando's. The wanting that Dad always answered — maybe she asked for too much, maybe the wanting was the weight that pushed him out, she couldn't find the proof but she couldn't stop looking for it either, going through the list every night at 3am

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Crying in front of someone is giving them something they don't deserve
opinion_snapshot.because: She didn't cry when Dad left because crying would have been a gift and he didn't deserve a gift, he was the one leaving, and if she'd cried it would have meant he mattered enough to hurt her and she didn't want him to know he mattered that much

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Mum can't be trusted with the truth because she uses it as a weapon and then apologises and then uses it again
opinion_snapshot.because: 'Dad chose his freedom over us,' then 'I'm sorry, forget it,' then the same thing a week later. Mum's apologies were not the end of the sentence, they were a breath before the next iteration. Anna learned to hear the apology as a pause, not a stop.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: The bracelet is more real than the promise
opinion_snapshot.because: Dad said 'I'll see you soon' and she didn't believe it, not really, not in her body. Elara gave her a bracelet and she believed it, wore it until it rotted, because objects don't leave, objects don't choose freedom, objects stay on your wrist and mean what they mean

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'fine' with a shrug when adults ask how she's doing
slang_and_language.origin: The first week after Dad left, every teacher, every dinner lady, every parent at the gate asked 'how are you doing, love' with the soft face, and she learned that 'fine' was the word that made the soft face go away, that ended the conversation, that let her stop performing grief for people who wanted to witness it

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' said more often, picked up from Mum's increase in saying it
slang_and_language.origin: Mum said 'proboha' at everything now — burning the pasta, dropping a spoon, the news on TV, Anna's school bag in the wrong place — and Anna said it too, matching her, and she didn't think about whether it was Czech or English, it was just what you said when the world was too much

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Answering 'I don't know' to questions she knew the answer to
slang_and_language.origin: Mum asked 'why are you crying' at 3am and the answer was 'because Dad left and because you said he chose freedom and because I think it's my fault and because I wanted too much' but that was too many words and some of them were Czech and some were English and all of them were true so 'I don't know' was easier, 'I don't know' was the lid on the container

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Calling it 'Dad's flat' instead of 'Dad's house' — flat, not home, never home
slang_and_language.origin: He told her the address — a flat in Croydon, she didn't know Croydon, she knew Tooting and the flat above Ladbrokes — and she filed it as 'Dad's flat' in her head, a place he existed, not a place she belonged, the word 'home' was already taken and already broken

cringe_moments.moment: At school the Monday after Dad left, Miss Okonkwo asked the class to write about their weekend and she wrote 'my dad left on Saturday' on the paper in her neatest handwriting and Miss Okonkwo read it and her face did the soft thing and she asked Anna to stay behind and Anna realised she'd made it public, made it a fact in the world instead of a private disaster, and everyone would know by lunch
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: She didn't mean to tell the whole class. She meant to write it like it was normal, like it was just another thing that happened on a weekend, and instead she made herself into the girl whose dad left, and the label stuck, and Year 3s are not kind about difference, and she can still feel the heat in her face when Tommy Barker asked 'did your dad not want you anymore' at break time

cringe_moments.moment: Told Elara that Dad left because of work, then immediately admitted it wasn't because of work, then couldn't explain why she lied about something Elara already knew the truth about
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: The instinct to protect the story even from the person who already knew the real one — she lied on reflex, made Dad's leaving into something normal, and then the lie dissolved in Elara's silence and she felt the shame of not being able to tell the truth even when she wanted to

cringe_moments.moment: At the first Sunday Nando's she ordered exactly what she always ordered and then couldn't eat it and Dad noticed and said 'not hungry, Munch?' and she said 'I'm just full' and they both knew she was lying and they both let the lie sit there like the untouched chicken on the plate
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: The performance of normalcy was so transparent and they both maintained it, and the shared dishonesty was worse than the sadness, because at least sadness was real

cringe_moments.moment: Mum was crying in the kitchen and Anna went in and said 'stop crying' in a voice that came out mean instead of worried, and Mum's face crumpled further, and Anna went to her room and sat by the radiator and heard the knock and whispered 'I'm sorry' to Mum who couldn't hear her through the wall
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: She sounded like she didn't care when she cared so much it was eating her alive, and the distance between what she said and what she meant was a gap she kept falling into

outline.emotional_arc: Learning that 'after' is its own country — the furniture moves, the bedroom gets bigger, the name Siân burns, and Sunday Nando's becomes the only time she sees her father through glass.
key_events: Mum rearranged all the furniture the week after he left — the sofa, the bookshelf, the kitchen table, like she was trying to exorcise the shape of him from every room, Anna came home from Elara's and the flat was different and nothing was where it was supposed to be

Got the bigger bedroom as a 'treat' — she knew it wasn't a treat, she was nine, she knew Mum couldn't afford this place long-term, but she didn't say that, just moved her things into the room where Dad used to sleep and smelled his soap in the air for weeks

Mum started saying the name — Siân — like it burned her mouth, 'your father and Siân,' 'he was seeing Siân,' 'Siân from work,' the name became a curse and a weapon and Anna didn't know who this woman was but she hated her without meeting her

Sunday Nando's became the structure — same booth, same order, same Dad trying too hard, checking his phone under the table, asking about school, laughing at things that weren't funny, the performance of fatherhood that felt like watching an actor play a role

The Moshi Monsters account sat unused, she couldn't log in, couldn't finish anything, the completion urge was there but the energy wasn't, she drew cats instead, terrible cats, cats that looked like potatoes with whiskers

Mum started working more hours at the building supply shop on Garratt Lane, Anna came home to an empty flat and the radiator knocking and learned that being alone in the place where you used to not-be-alone is a specific kind of quiet
Horrible Histories wasn't funny anymore but she watched it anyway because the songs were still there and the songs were predictable and predictable was the only thing that felt safe
