new_or_changed_relationships: Marcus — every Sunday she waited for him to cancel, he never did, but the waiting was its own exhaustion, the relief when he showed up was always followed by the dread of when he'd leave again, Nando's became a place of ritual and hollow joy

Klára — Mum's grief became anger became explanations Anna didn't ask for, 'he was seeing her before he left, you know,' 'I gave up everything for him,' Anna learned to nod and not ask questions because questions made Mum cry and Mum crying made Anna feel responsible

Siân — a name without a face, the enemy, the reason, the woman who existed in the space where her family used to be, Anna drew her in her head as a villain without features, just a shape that took things
outline.internal_state: The flat was the same flat but everything in it had moved. Her bedroom was bigger but it didn't feel like hers, it felt like a room she was borrowing from someone who wasn't coming back. Mum's anger was easier to handle than Mum's crying — anger had a direction, crying was just weather. She started picking at things more: the wallpaper near the bathroom, the skin around her thumbnails, the friendship bracelet on her wrist that was turning grey and frayed. The fear that she'd made him leave sat in her chest like the damp spot in the bathroom — growing, smelly, ignored. Nobody asked her how she was doing because Mum was the one who was wronged, Mum was the one who got to fall apart, Anna was just the kid who got moved into the bigger bedroom like that was supposed to fix it.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: School at Fircroft, walk home with the key Mum gave her on a string around her neck because Mum was on shift at the building supply shop until six now. Let herself in. The flat smelled different since the rearrange — the sofa had been where the bookshelf was and the bookshelf had been where the kitchen table was and nothing smelled like itself anymore, just like furniture polish and Mum's desperate energy. She'd drop her bag by the door — not the hook, the floor, Mum stopped correcting her about the hook — and turn on CBBC. Horrible Histories on iPlayer, the same episodes, the songs first because the songs were the predictable part. She knew all the words to the Viking song but didn't sing anymore, just mouthed them while picking at the skin around her left thumbnail until it bled a little, then switched to the right one. The radiator knocked at half four and she flinched and nobody saw her flinch because nobody was there. Sometimes she drew cats at the kitchen table — not the table that used to be there, the new table, the one that didn't have Dad's elbow mark on it. The cats were terrible. She knew they were terrible. She drew them anyway because the Moshi Monsters account was locked and she couldn't remember the password and Mum didn't know how to reset it and she couldn't finish the collection that was almost done, 97%, and the incompleteness sat in her chest like a stone. At 5:45 she'd hear Mum's key in the door and the smell of the shop would come in — sawdust and that specific building-supply smell, PVC windows and rubber sealant — and Mum would say 'ahoj, zlatíčko' and Anna would say 'hi' back, always English, always the wrong language, and Mum's face would do the thing and then they'd have tea and Mum would sometimes talk about Siân without starting the conversation, just dropping the name like a hot coal into whatever they were discussing. Sundays were Nando's. Same booth. Same order. Same performance.

key_scenes.location: The flat above Ladbrokes, Garratt Lane, the sitting room
key_scenes.what_happened: She came home from Elara Chung's flat at 4pm on a Wednesday — she thinks it was Wednesday, it was definitely after school — and the sitting room was wrong. The sofa was against the window wall now. The bookshelf blocked the radiator. The kitchen table was where the sofa had been and the two chairs were on opposite sides like they'd been placed there by someone who didn't eat at the table anymore. Mum was still moving things — the TV was unplugged, the cord trailing across the carpet like a dead snake. 'Do you like it better?' Mum asked, and her voice was bright and fake, and there were sweat stains under her arms, and she'd been doing this all day while Anna was at Elara's, moving everything so nothing was where it had been when he was here. Anna said 'it's fine' because 'fine' was what you said when you were nine and your home had rearranged itself overnight. She went to her bedroom and the bedroom was the same but the sounds were different now — the sofa used to block the radiator knock and now nothing did and the man inside was louder.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Disorientation that felt like seasickness. The flat was the same square footage but it was a different country. Nothing was where her body expected it to be. She reached for the light switch by the bathroom door and it wasn't there — it was on the other side now, because the bookshelf had been moved and rewired or something, she didn't understand, she just knew her hand found wall instead of switch and that small failure made her want to scream.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The sound of the TV cord dragging across the carpet — a thick, resistant scraping — and Mum's breathing, fast and determined, like she was running a race against something she couldn't name.

key_scenes.location: The flat above Ladbrokes, Garratt Lane, the spare room that became Anna's room
key_scenes.what_happened: Mum said it was a treat. 'The bigger bedroom — you can have it now. Your own space.' She said it like Anna had won something. Anna looked at the room and saw Dad's side of the bed, the dent in the pillow, the empty space on the nightstand where his phone charger used to be. Mum had stripped the sheets but the mattress was the same and the wardrobe was the same and the curtains were the same dark blue that made the room feel like evening even at noon. She moved her things in on Saturday — her Peppa Pig DVD box even though she didn't watch it anymore, her stone collection in the Nike shoebox, the friendship bracelet from Elara Chung that she'd stopped wearing but couldn't throw away. The room smelled like Dad's soap for three weeks. Imperial Leather, the red one, the one with the little stamp in the centre. She'd lie in bed and breathe it in and feel like she was lying inside a memory of him, and the radiator pipe in this room was even louder because it was the pipe that ran from the kitchen, and she could hear Mum's voice through it sometimes, talking on the phone in Czech, saying Siân, saying proboha, saying things Anna translated in fragments.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: The guilt of inheriting something that was taken from someone else. This was his room. His bed. His smell still in it. She was sleeping in his absence and calling it a bedroom. The space was bigger but she felt smaller in it — like the room was a container that had been made for a different shape and she was the wrong thing inside it.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The Imperial Leather smell — not the whole bar, just the ghost of it, embedded in the wardrobe wood — and the way it faded week by week until one morning she couldn't find it anymore and she grieved the smell like it was a person.

key_scenes.location: Nando's on Garratt Lane, the booth by the window
key_scenes.what_happened: Sunday. The booth by the window. Same one. He was already there when they arrived — Mum didn't come in anymore, just dropped her at the door like a handoff, like a package being delivered. He stood up when she walked in and said 'alright, Munch?' and she said 'alright' and they sat down and he asked how school was and she said 'fine' and he asked what she'd been up to and she said 'nothing' and the silence stretched like toffee. He ordered the usual — 1/4 chicken, medium, with chips and corn on the cob, plus the olives she didn't eat but he always ordered because he'd forgotten she didn't eat olives, or maybe he'd never known. His phone buzzed and he glanced at it under the table — she saw the screen light reflect on his chin — and he typed something quick, one-handed, not looking at her while he typed. She knew it was Siân. She didn't know how she knew. She just knew. He put the phone face-down and smiled and asked about Horrible Histories and she told him about the Viking song and he said 'that's class' in that way he said things when he wasn't listening, and she watched him perform being her dad and she was nine and she could see the performance, the effort, the checking-the-watch-when-he-thought-she-wasn't-looking, and she hated him for it and loved him and wanted him to cancel so she wouldn't have to sit in this booth pretending anymore.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: The exhaustion of waiting for him to leave. The relief when he arrived was already being eaten by the dread of when he'd go. Every Sunday was a countdown. Every 'alright, Munch?' was a line in a script. She wanted to say 'stop calling me Munch' and 'who's Siân' and 'why does your phone matter more than me' but instead she ate her chips one at a time and picked the skin off the chicken and said 'fine' and 'nothing' and 'yeah' and let the silence fill up the space where a conversation should be.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The orange Nando's sauce on his fingertips and the way he licked it off without thinking, like he was still a person who existed outside of this performance, like somewhere in him was still the dad who used to put her on his shoulders and walk down Garratt Lane like the world was giving them things.

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: He became someone she watched instead of someone she was with. The Sunday visits were structured, rehearsed, full of questions he asked because he was supposed to, not because he wanted the answers. She started tracking his attention — when he checked his phone, when his eyes drifted to the TV above the counter, when he said 'that's class' about something she'd said that wasn't class-worthy, it was just a thing, and the emptiness of his praise made her stop sharing. She protected herself by expecting less. By the time summer came, she'd perfected the art of filling silences with chip-eating so he wouldn't feel obligated to talk, and she wasn't sure if that was kindness or surrender.

Mum's grief mutated into information Anna never asked for. 'He was seeing her before, you know.' 'I gave up everything for him — my country, my family, everything.' 'Siân from work. That's where he was on the late shifts.' Each sentence was a brick in a wall Anna hadn't agreed to build. She learned to nod without listening, to make her face go blank, to not ask follow-up questions because questions made Mum cry and Mum crying was worse than Mum talking. The Saturday routine survived but the Sainsbury's café booth felt different now — Mum's face was emptier, the magazine was a prop, and the silence between them was full of things they both knew but wouldn't say. Anna became the container for Mum's anger and she didn't consent to this role but there was no one else to fill it.

She became real. Not a person — Anna still hadn't seen her face — but a fact. A force. A name that lived in the flat like mould, growing in the corners of conversations, appearing without warning. 'Your father and Siân.' 'Siân's probably got a nice flat.' 'Siân this, Siân that.' Anna drew her in her head: tall, thin, mean-looking, the kind of woman who took things. The villain in a story that was supposed to be about her family. She didn't know yet that hating someone you've never met is exhausting. She just knew the name made her stomach clench and her hand reach for the skin around her thumbnail.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to be alone in a place that used to have people in it
knowledge_acquired.how: Mum's shifts at the building supply shop meant Anna came home to an empty flat three days a week. The key on the string. The sound of her own footsteps on the carpet. The radiator knocking at half four with no one to see her flinch. She learned to fill the silence with CBBC and the silence underneath the CBBC was a different silence than the one that used to live in the flat — it was a chosen silence, not a shared one, and chosen silence felt like control but it also felt like being forgotten.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to watch someone perform love
knowledge_acquired.how: Sunday Nando's. Every week. Marcus trying too hard — the questions, the laughter at nothing, the 'alright, Munch?' that came out automatic. She started recognising the difference between real attention and performed attention. Real attention was when he forgot to check his phone. Performed attention was everything else. She catalogued his performances the way she used to catalogue stickers — noting each instance, rating its authenticity, building a collection of evidence that the dad she remembered was becoming a character he played on Sundays.

knowledge_acquired.thing: That grief makes people tell children things children shouldn't hear
knowledge_acquired.how: Mum's unfiltered mouth. The details about Siân. The details about the marriage. The way Mum spoke like Anna was a friend at a pub instead of a nine-year-old eating Weetabix. She learned that adults in pain don't edit. She learned to edit for them — nodding, not asking, absorbing, containing. She didn't know the word 'parentification' yet but she was living inside it.

knowledge_acquired.thing: The Horrible Histories songs by heart — all of them, even the ones from seasons she didn't like
knowledge_acquired.how: The after-school routine. CBBC iPlayer on loop. The songs were predictable and predictability was the only currency that spent right anymore. She didn't watch the episodes for the history — she watched for the structure. Verse, chorus, verse, chorus, punchline. The reliable shape of it. She mouthed the words and picked her thumbnails and let the songs fill the flat where the quiet used to live.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: The bigger bedroom is a punishment wearing a treat's clothes
opinion_snapshot.because: Mum said it was a treat but the room smelled like Dad and the wardrobe still had his hangers in it and moving into someone's absence isn't a gift, it's an eviction notice with nicer handwriting

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Siân is responsible even though Anna has never met her
opinion_snapshot.because: Mum says the name like a curse and the name appears when the crying starts and the name is the reason the flat got rearranged and the reason Dad checks his phone under the table and the reason Sunday Nando's feels like a meeting instead of a meal. Siân is the cause. Siân is the villain. Anna doesn't need to meet her to know this.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Sunday Nando's is a lie they're both telling
opinion_snapshot.because: He performs being her dad. She performs being okay. The food is the same and the booth is the same but nothing that matters is the same and they both know it and neither of them says it. The lie is the point. Without the lie, there's no reason to meet.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Mum is using her as a bin for feelings that aren't hers
opinion_snapshot.because: The things Mum says about Dad and Siân — those are adult things. Those are things for a therapist or a friend or a journal. Not for a nine-year-old eating toast. Anna didn't agree to carry this. She didn't consent. But there's no form to sign, no box to tick, no way to opt out of being the audience for your mother's grief.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Being alone after school is better than being alone with someone who's crying
opinion_snapshot.because: The empty flat is quiet but it's her quiet. She controls the TV. She controls the cats she draws. She controls whether the radiator knock makes her flinch. When Mum is home and crying, the flat belongs to the crying. When Mum is at work, the flat belongs to Anna. Ownership matters when you're nine and everything else has been taken.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'fine' and 'nothing' and 'yeah' as complete sentences
slang_and_language.origin: Sunday Nando's. The questions Dad asked that she stopped really answering because the answers didn't land anywhere, they just fell into the space between them and dissolved. 'Fine' was a wall. 'Nothing' was a door she closed. 'Yeah' was whatever he needed to hear so he'd stop trying.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: The name 'Siân' said internally with a flinch, like a word that bites
slang_and_language.origin: Mum's mouth. The way Klára said it — 'Siân' — with the sharpness on the S and the flatness on the â, like the word itself was the problem. Anna absorbed the flinch. Now the name lives in her body as a small electric shock, even when she just thinks it.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' said with Mum's exact cadence, same situations, same tiredness
slang_and_language.origin: The fridge bump. The dropped keys. The shopping bag splitting. Anna said it at school once — the dinner lady gave her a weird look — and realised she'd been speaking Czech without noticing, been channelling Mum without choosing to, been becoming the thing she watched.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Mouthing Horrible Histories lyrics instead of singing them
slang_and_language.origin: The flat was too quiet for singing. Singing filled the silence and then the silence came back worse. Mouthing was safe. Mouthing was private. Mouthing was having the song without giving it to the room, because the room might take it and make it mean something else.

cringe_moments.moment: At Nando's, she asked Dad if Siân was pretty and he went silent for five full seconds before saying 'that's not — why would you — who told you that name?' and she realised Mum had been feeding her information that Dad didn't know she had, and the silence was him recalculating everything, and she'd broken the unspoken rule that Sundays were for pretending the rest of the week didn't exist.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: She saw his face rearrange. She saw him understand that Mum was telling her things. She became a witness and a weapon at the same time and she didn't consent to either role. The silence lasted five seconds. She's counted it a thousand times.

cringe_moments.moment: She drew a picture of Siân — a tall, thin, ugly woman with sharp teeth — and left it on the kitchen table and Mum saw it and didn't laugh, didn't get angry, just went very quiet and said 'she's not a monster, Anna' and then went to the bathroom and turned the tap on and Anna sat at the table with her terrible drawing and felt like the worst person alive for making Mum defend the woman who'd destroyed their family.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Mum defending Siân. That was worse than anything. If Mum defended her, maybe Siân wasn't the villain. Maybe the villain was more complicated. Maybe the villain was Dad. Maybe the villain was nobody. Anna didn't want to live in a world where nobody was the villain because that meant nobody was coming to fix it.

cringe_moments.moment: She told a girl at school — Harriet Price, who asked why her dad didn't pick her up anymore — that her dad was dead, and then had to un-tell it the next day when Harriet's mum called Klára to offer condolences, and Klára's face when she got off the phone was something Anna had never seen before — not angry, not sad, just empty, like she'd used up all her reactions on bigger things and had none left for this small stupid lie her daughter told because it was easier than the truth.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: She chose death because it was simpler. Death has no Siân. Death has no Sunday Nando's. Death has no performance. She wanted the clean version of the story and she invented it and then she had to un-invent it and the un-inventing was worse than the lying because everyone knew she'd lied and she could feel them knowing.

cringe_moments.moment: At Elara Chung's flat, Elara's mum asked how her dad was doing and Anna said 'he's fine, he's just really busy with work' and Elara gave her a look — not pity, just recognition — and Anna realised Elara knew it was a lie and was letting her tell it anyway, and the kindness of that made Anna want to cry, and she didn't cry, she just sat there with her noodles going cold and her throat closed and the friendship bracelet on her wrist feeling like a tourniquet.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Being seen lying is worse than being caught lying. Elara saw her and didn't expose her and the not-exposing was love but it was also proof that the lie was visible, that it wasn't holding, that everyone could see through it.
